title: How To Use Facial Anatomy To Make Your Injection Technique Safer!
filename: How To Use Facial Anatomy To Make Your Injection Technique Safer!.en.vtt
excerpt: And what I was tryin courage people to do is at the point of injection to get the core anatomy in the forefront of their mind sometime there's a lot to think about at that point at that moment where you're doing to put the needle in but that should be the last thing that goes thr…

segments.text: and what I was tryin courage people to do is at the point of injection to get the core anatomy in the forefront of their mind sometime there's a lot to think about at that point at that moment where you're doing to put the needle in but that should be the last thing that goes through your head and that's the early stages of making the decision using an athame to inform your decisions as opposed to just thinking about injection technique because sometimes people get too overwhelmed with the needle and the plunger and the patient's response is a heck of a lot to think about in that moment but the last thing should be well actually is not the last thing because you should plan your injection first around the anatomy so where's the artery where's a fat pad what am I likely to touch when I'm in the right place like often you're aiming for the periosteum so at the point where you touched the bone what angle should you be at and then you should be shaping that almost and wasn't it's navigating is it so that as you angling the needle in you're imagining is a getting closer or further away from the artery I'm trying to avoid with the

angle that I'm choosing and the more you use that the more certain you feel so if you for example take the nasolabial fold injection risky injection runs facial artery runs in your cheek dips into the nasolabial fold and some people like the Loch Ness monster and if you're angling down to touch the periosteum but you're angled for those of you can't see me on in audio and pointing slightly up towards as if I was sliding the needle in it would be heading towards my the opposite sides cheekbone at that angle I'm getting slightly closer to where the artery might be meeting the nose so if I change the angle slightly less of it so if I keep so that it's now pointing at the mid cheek and the opposite side of my face I'm gonna be slightly safer and maybe slightly more the other way I'd be slightly further away from that artery even more so the angle when you know where the artery is you know how your angle of entry affects your proximity to that artery and that that's an example of using the anatomy to change your technique slightly it's tiny changes it's barely

perceptible but when you think about it a lot it becomes intuitive I know the artery is I know where I don't want my needle tip to end I know what I'm gonna touch when I when I'm in the right place now I go for it with all those thoughts in my mind I hit the right place I'm ready to inject after I've aspirated and that's the process you wanted to get to when your Anatomy isn't ironed out and you only know the name of the artery that's a very uncertain feeling like it is you're going into the dark now obviously everyone's were always there's always an element of the dark unfortunately because you never ever each individual is different but that the more light you can shed on it the more confident you feel and more accurately or you'll be [Music]
